refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct obligations: (1) deriving requirements from mission context, (2) recording them, and (3) managing them over time. Atomicity requires separating these concerns. CR-NGHE-005.1 focuses on the derivation act—the functional obligation to transform worksite inputs (mission, constraints, safety plan, operational policies) into a set of performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability). Recording and management are separate functional concerns addressed in sub-FRs. The FR remains in the problem domain (what must be derived) without prescribing how derivation occurs (algorithm, tool, manual process).
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that given a documented worksite mission context (site characteristics, operational policies, safety constraints), the system produces a traceable set of performance targets that satisfy acceptance criteria: each target is linked to at least one worksite input, and the derivation logic is documented and reviewable by the Site Manager and System Engineer.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.2: The NGHE solution shall record derived performance requirements with traceability to worksite inputs.
CR-NGHE-005.3: The NGHE solution shall maintain a history of performance requirement baselines and calibration updates.
CR-NGHE-005.4: The NGHE solution shall provide access to current and historical performance requirements to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, Safety Manager, Maintenance/Quality).

candidate_dps: DP-NGHE-005.1a: Establish a parameterized mission-context intake process that captures worksite operational profile (hazard class, duty cycle, environmental conditions, safety constraints) and maps each input to corresponding performance target dimensions (reliability, response time, accuracy, safety margin) via a documented traceability matrix.

DP-NGHE-005.1b: Implement a baseline performance specification repository indexed by worksite mission class, with automated derivation rules that compute target values from mission inputs and version-control all baseline updates with audit trail.

DP-NGHE-005.1c: Design a two-stage requirement flow: (1) ingest worksite safety plan and operational policies as structured constraints; (2) apply constraint-satisfaction logic to synthesize performance targets, with explicit documentation of which policy element drove each target.

DP-NGHE-005.1d: Establish a performance target derivation engine that accepts worksite mission context as input, applies domain-specific heuristics (e.g., safety-critical operations require higher reliability margins), and outputs a documented performance specification with full traceability back to source constraints.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-NGHE-005.1d: Establish a performance target derivation engine that accepts worksite mission context as input, applies domain-specific heuristics (e.g., safety-critical operations require higher reliability margins), and outputs a documented performance specification with full traceability back to source constraints.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between mission-context intake and derivation logic: mitigate by separating input schema (IM layer) from heuristic rules (IM layer), allowing independent evolution.
Dependency on external safety plan and operational policy documents: mitigate by defining a formal interface contract (schema/API) for policy inputs, decoupling NGHE from policy authoring tools.
Assumption that domain heuristics are stable and complete: risk that new mission classes or hazard types require heuristic updates; mitigate via extensible rule framework.
coupling_check.notes: DP-NGHE-005.1d is preferred because it explicitly names the derivation engine as the logical solution concept, avoiding premature technology binding while supporting modularity. Candidates 005.1a and 005.1b are more prescriptive (repository, matrix) and risk over-constraining the IM layer. Candidate 005.1c is sound but less actionable without specifying the derivation mechanism.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Worksite mission context is available in structured or semi-structured form at system initialization. Risk: If context is implicit or evolves during operation, derivation engine may produce stale targets. Mitigation: Define a context-update protocol and re-derivation trigger conditions.

Assumption: Domain heuristics for mapping mission inputs to performance targets are well-defined and validated. Risk: Heuristics may be incomplete, biased, or conflict with safety standards. Mitigation: Require heuristic validation against historical worksite data and regulatory benchmarks before deployment.

Assumption: Traceability matrix and audit trail can be maintained without significant performance overhead. Risk: Large-scale worksites with frequent policy updates may generate audit bloat. Mitigation: Implement tiered logging (summary + detailed) and archival strategy.

Assumption: Safety plan and operational policies are authored in a format compatible with the derivation engine's input schema. Risk: Legacy or non-standard policy documents may require manual translation. Mitigation: Provide policy-to-schema translation tools or templates.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR-NGHE-005.1 to explicitly state: 'The NGHE solution shall provide a derivation mechanism that transforms documented worksite mission context (hazard classification, duty cycle, environmental constraints, safety policies) into a set of performance targets, with full traceability to source inputs and version-controlled baseline history.' This clarifies the logical boundary (derivation engine) and acceptance criteria (traceability + versioning), enabling cleaner IM-layer decomposition and reducing ambiguity about what 'derives' means in practice.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized performance target derivation engine that ingests worksite mission context, applies domain-specific safety and reliability heuristics, and outputs documented performance specifications with full traceability to source constraints.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives mission context (worksite parameters, operational mode, safety classification) from task planning layer

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs derived performance specification (reliability margins, safety thresholds, operational limits) to vehicle and teleoperation subsystems

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional: receives safety constraint updates; broadcasts performance target changes when heuristics trigger new margin requirements

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: performance target derivation is fundamentally a safety-critical function. Domain-specific heuristics (safety margins, reliability thresholds) are native to Safety Services. Traceability and constraint mapping align with safety assurance responsibilities. Minor diffusion: outputs must propagate to Vehicle and Teleoperation via Telemetry interface, but this is expected cross-module communication, not algorithmic diffusion.

Too broad and abstract. NGHE Solution is a top-level container; placing a concrete derivation engine here violates separation of concerns. The engine would need to diffuse logic into Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services to be operationally useful. No clear ownership or accountability.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical/operational subsystem, not a policy or specification layer. Performance target derivation is a meta-level function that must remain independent of vehicle-specific implementation. Placing it here couples specification logic to hardware, making it difficult to reuse heuristics across different vehicle types or missions. High diffusion to Teleoperation and Safety Services.

Weak fit: Teleoperation is an operational control interface, not a specification authority. Performance targets must be derived before teleoperation begins; placing derivation here creates circular dependency (operator inputs mission context, but operator also needs performance targets to guide control). High diffusion to Vehicle and Safety Services.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the natural owner of performance target derivation because: (1) domain-specific heuristics are safety-centric (reliability margins, fault tolerance thresholds); (2) traceability to source constraints is a core safety assurance function; (3) the engine operates at the specification layer, not the operational layer; (4) outputs (performance specs) are safety-critical artifacts that must be governed by safety authority; (5) external interfaces (Tasking in, Telemetry/SafetyAlert out) align with Safety Services' role as a cross-cutting assurance function.
containment_risks: Risk: If mission context from Tasking is incomplete or ambiguous, Safety Services may derive overly conservative or incorrect performance targets. Mitigation: establish formal contract on Tasking interface specifying required mission context fields and validation rules.

Risk: Performance targets derived by Safety Services may conflict with Vehicle or Teleoperation operational constraints not visible at derivation time. Mitigation: implement feedback loop via SafetyAlert to allow Vehicle/Teleoperation to flag infeasible targets; revise heuristics iteratively.

Risk: Traceability chain may break if source constraints are not formally captured in a constraint registry accessible to Safety Services. Mitigation: establish a shared constraint repository (e.g., requirements database) that Safety Services queries during derivation.

Risk: Multiple mission types or vehicle classes may require different heuristic sets; monolithic engine in Safety Services could become unmaintainable. Mitigation: design engine as a pluggable heuristic framework with mission/vehicle-specific rule sets loaded at runtime.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-NGHE-005.1d correctly maps to FR-NGHE-005.1. The DP specifies a concrete solution (performance target derivation engine with domain-specific heuristics and traceability) that directly addresses the functional requirement (derive performance requirements from worksite mission context). The DP is sufficiently detailed to guide design and includes explicit outputs (documented performance specification with traceability), which are verifiable against acceptance criteria.

diffusion_risks: Minor diffusion risk: Performance target outputs propagate via Telemetry interface to Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems. While this is expected cross-module communication (not algorithmic diffusion), the interface contract must be formally specified to prevent Vehicle/Teleoperation from re-implementing derivation logic locally.

Moderate diffusion risk: If mission context from Tasking interface is incomplete or poorly specified, Safety Services may need to infer missing parameters, potentially duplicating logic that should reside in Tasking. Mitigation: establish formal contract on Tasking interface with required mission context fields and validation rules.

Moderate diffusion risk: Heuristic rules may become scattered across multiple rule sets if mission/vehicle-specific variants are not managed as a pluggable framework. Risk of inconsistent derivation logic across different operational modes. Mitigation: design engine as a parameterized heuristic framework with externalized rule sets.

Low diffusion risk: Feedback loop via SafetyAlert (inbound constraint updates) could create circular dependency if Vehicle/Teleoperation attempt to re-derive targets based on operational feedback. Mitigation: establish clear ownership: Safety Services owns derivation; Vehicle/Teleoperation own feasibility feedback only.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services is the correct IM owner (containment score 0.92). The module boundary is well-defined: it owns the derivation engine, heuristic logic, and traceability mapping. External interfaces are appropriate: Tasking (inbound mission context), Telemetry (outbound performance specs), SafetyAlert (bidirectional constraint updates). The module operates at the specification/policy layer, not the operational layer, which is correct for a meta-level function. No algorithmic diffusion detected; outputs are properly routed via defined interfaces. Ownership and accountability are clear.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criteria are directly verifiable: (1) 'documented set of performance targets traceable to worksite inputs' can be verified by inspecting Safety Services outputs and tracing each target back to source mission context via the traceability mechanism; (2) 'maintains a history of baselines/calibration updates' can be verified by auditing Safety Services' internal state management and version control. Both criteria are testable artifacts produced by the IM boundary.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR-NGHE-005.1 is allocated to Safety Services via DP-NGHE-005.1d. The allocation is unambiguous: Safety Services owns the derivation engine, heuristics, and traceability logic. Input/output interfaces are explicitly defined (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert). Responsibilities are separated from Vehicle (operational execution) and Teleoperation (control interface), avoiding duplication. The allocation respects the safety-critical nature of performance target derivation and aligns with Safety Services' role as a cross-cutting assurance function.

recommended_revisions: Formalize the Tasking interface contract: specify required mission context fields (worksite parameters, operational mode, safety classification), validation rules, and error handling for incomplete inputs. This prevents Safety Services from inferring missing parameters and reduces diffusion risk.

Design the heuristic engine as a pluggable framework: externalize domain-specific rules (safety margins, reliability thresholds, fault tolerance policies) into mission/vehicle-specific rule sets loaded at runtime. This prevents monolithic growth and enables reuse across different operational contexts.

Establish a shared constraint registry (e.g., requirements database or configuration service) that Safety Services queries during derivation. This ensures traceability chain integrity and provides a single source of truth for source constraints. Document the registry interface and access patterns.

Define the SafetyAlert feedback loop explicitly: specify what types of constraint updates trigger re-derivation, what constitutes an 'infeasible target' from Vehicle/Teleoperation, and the approval workflow for performance target changes. This prevents circular dependencies and maintains Safety Services' authority over derivation.

Implement version control and audit logging for performance target baselines and calibration updates. This satisfies the acceptance criterion 'maintains a history of baselines/calibration updates' and provides traceability for safety assurance.

Add a formal interface specification for Telemetry outputs: document the performance specification schema (reliability margins, safety thresholds, operational limits), update frequency, and notification protocol. This ensures Vehicle and Teleoperation can reliably consume derived targets without re-implementing derivation logic.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP is sufficiently detailed and the IM allocation is clear and well-contained. However, the recommended revisions reveal critical design dependencies that must be resolved at the next decomposition level to mitigate diffusion risks and ensure safety-critical correctness. Specifically: (1) the Tasking interface contract is underspecified, creating risk of parameter inference duplication; (2) the heuristic engine architecture lacks explicit pluggability, risking monolithic growth and inconsistent rule application; (3) the constraint registry and SafetyAlert feedback loop lack formal specifications, creating circular dependency risks. These are not defects in the current FR-DP-IM mapping, but rather architectural details that belong in the next level of decomposition. Decomposing FR-NGHE-005.1 into sub-FRs will force explicit design of these interfaces and mechanisms, reducing diffusion risk and improving safety assurance. High safety criticality justifies this deeper decomposition.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-005.1.1: Establish and validate mission context from Tasking interface (required fields, format, completeness checks, error handling for missing parameters)
FR-NGHE-005.1.2: Derive performance targets using pluggable domain-specific heuristic rules (load mission/vehicle-specific rule sets, apply derivation logic, generate performance specification)

FR-NGHE-005.1.3: Maintain traceability chain from source mission context through heuristic application to derived performance targets (record derivation path, source constraints, rule application sequence)
FR-NGHE-005.1.4: Manage performance target baselines and calibration history (version control, audit logging, baseline snapshots, change approval workflow)

FR-NGHE-005.1.5: Process constraint updates and feasibility feedback from Vehicle/Teleoperation via SafetyAlert (identify infeasible targets, trigger re-derivation, maintain Safety Services authority over derivation decisions)

revise.why: No revisions needed at current level. FR-NGHE-005.1 and DP-NGHE-005.1d are correctly specified. Recommended revisions are architectural details that belong in sub-level decomposition, not corrections to the current mapping.

assumptions: Tasking interface will provide complete mission context; if incomplete, Safety Services must not infer missing parameters but instead reject and escalate.
Heuristic rules can be externalized and parameterized without loss of safety assurance; rule framework design will be addressed in sub-level decomposition.
Constraint registry exists or will be designed as a shared service; Safety Services will query it during derivation.
SafetyAlert feedback loop is unidirectional in authority: Vehicle/Teleoperation provide feasibility feedback only; Safety Services retain authority over re-derivation decisions.
Performance target baselines are immutable once approved; changes require formal approval workflow.
High safety criticality justifies detailed interface specifications and audit logging at next decomposition level.
open_risks: Tasking interface contract underspecification: if mission context fields are not formally defined, Safety Services may infer missing parameters, duplicating logic that should reside in Tasking. Mitigation: decompose FR-NGHE-005.1.1 to force explicit interface design.

Heuristic rule diffusion: if domain-specific rules are not managed as a pluggable framework, they may scatter across multiple rule sets or become embedded in derivation logic, creating inconsistency and maintenance burden. Mitigation: decompose FR-NGHE-005.1.2 to force pluggable architecture design.

Circular dependency in SafetyAlert feedback loop: if Vehicle/Teleoperation attempt to re-derive targets based on operational feedback, circular dependency and authority confusion may result. Mitigation: decompose FR-NGHE-005.1.5 to force explicit feedback loop specification and authority boundaries.

Traceability chain integrity: if derivation path is not explicitly recorded, traceability may be lost, compromising safety assurance and auditability. Mitigation: decompose FR-NGHE-005.1.3 to force explicit traceability mechanism design.

Baseline version control: if performance target baselines are not versioned and audited, history requirement cannot be verified. Mitigation: decompose FR-NGHE-005.1.4 to force version control and audit logging design.
evidence_needed: Formal Tasking interface specification: required mission context fields, data types, validation rules, error handling protocol, completeness checks.
Heuristic engine architecture design: pluggable rule framework, rule set loading mechanism, parameterization strategy, rule application sequence, extensibility points.
Constraint registry interface specification: query API, constraint schema, update protocol, access control, consistency guarantees.
SafetyAlert feedback loop specification: inbound constraint update types, re-derivation triggers, approval workflow, authority boundaries, circular dependency prevention.
Version control and audit logging design: baseline snapshot mechanism, change history storage, audit log schema, retention policy, access control.
Performance specification schema: reliability margins, safety thresholds, operational limits, update frequency, notification protocol, consumption contract for Vehicle/Teleoperation.
